title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
text:
In modern agriculture, the transition from harvest to processing is a race against time and cost. This project focuses on the spatial representation of agricultural flow, mapping the transit corridors of three critical commodities: Maize, Soybeans, and Wheat.
By utilizing dynamic vector paths, we can visualize the connectivity between production hubs and export terminals, providing a clear picture of regional logistics integration.


Fragmented logistics data often hides the complexity of multi-commodity transport. Understanding the “path of least resistance” and the overlap of different crop routes is essential for: * Infrastructure Planning: Identifying high-traffic corridors that require maintenance. * Cost Mitigation: Optimizing haulage distances to reduce fuel consumption. * Risk Assessment: Visualizing dependency on specific transit bottlenecks.



This analysis implements advanced line-string visualization techniques: * Dynamic Flow Mapping (AntPath): Using animated polylines to indicate the direction and “pulse” of commodity movement. * Multivariate Color Coding: Assigning specific hues to different crops to analyze route overlap and specialization. * Spatial Intersection: Understanding how different supply chains converge at primary logistical nodes.



To ensure scalability and performance, the project utilizes the following stack:








Tool
Core Function
Application in Project




GeoPandas
Spatial Vector Engine
Construction and manipulation of LineString geometries for transit routes.


Folium (AntPath)
Animation Engine
Deployment of non-static, animated polylines to represent flow direction.


Branca
HTML/CSS Styling
Advanced customization of interactive legends and dashboard components.

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
text:
2 Network Acquisition and Topology Cleaning
In this stage, we utilize OSMNX to download the real-world road infrastructure from OpenStreetMap. A critical step in supply chain modeling is ensuring network connectivity; otherwise, routing algorithms would fail due to isolated road segments.

2.1 Graph Extraction and Connectivity Audit
We extract a driving network centered on a strategic agricultural hub. To ensure a valid model, we perform a Connected Components analysis to retain only the largest interconnected subgraph, eliminating any “dead-end” segments that aren’t physically linked to the main infrastructure.


Review Technical Code (Python)
import osmnx as ox
import networkx as nx
import folium
from folium.plugins import AntPath

# 1. Geographic Configuration (Agricultural Core Region)
# Centered near a strategic logistical node
center_coords = (-33.89, -60.57) 
print("Downloading road infrastructure from OpenStreetMap...")

# 2. Network Retrieval and Topology Refinement
# Downloads the road network within a 20km radius
G_raw = ox.graph_from_point(center_coords, dist=20000, network_type='drive')

# 3. Ensuring Graph Connectivity
# We convert to undirected to find connected components and extract the 
# Largest Strongly Connected Component (LSCC) to ensure routing feasibility.
G_weak = G_raw.to_undirected()
largest_component = max(nx.connected_components(G_weak), key=len)
G = G_raw.subgraph(largest_component).copy()

# 4. Critical Node Identification
# Locating the nearest network node to our primary processing facility (Plant)
target_plant_node = ox.nearest_nodes(G, center_coords[1], center_coords[0])

print(f"Network processing complete. Total nodes in analyzed subgraph: {len(G.nodes)}")


Downloading road infrastructure from OpenStreetMap...
Network processing complete. Total nodes in analyzed subgraph: 4350

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
text:
3 Supply Node Configuration: Farm-to-Plant Logistics
In this section, we define the Source Nodes (Farms) that represent the origin of the agricultural supply chain. Each node is characterized by its geographic coordinates, current storage volume in metric tons (MT), and the specific commodity being transported.

3.1 Commodity Segmentation
To visualize the multi-product flow, we categorized the supply into three strategic pillars: * Maize (Yellow): High-volume grain transport. * Soybeans (Green): Critical oilseed export commodity. * Wheat (Orange): Essential cereal for regional milling.


Review Technical Code (Python)
# Production Node Dataset (Off-road Origins)
# This dataset represents the supply nodes for the multi-commodity logistics model.
# NOTE: Farm names (Spanish) are preserved for regional context.

farms_data = [
    {"name": "Finca El Sol", "coords": (-33.80, -60.48), "ton": 80, "commodity": "Maize", "color": "#f1c40f"},
    {"name": "Don Pedro", "coords": (-33.97, -60.42), "ton": 40, "commodity": "Soybeans", "color": "#2ecc71"},
    {"name": "La Posta", "coords": (-33.83, -60.78), "ton": 120, "commodity": "Wheat", "color": "#e67e22"},
    {"name": "El Trébol", "coords": (-34.05, -60.58), "ton": 65, "commodity": "Maize", "color": "#f1c40f"},
    {"name": "Santa Rita", "coords": (-33.75, -60.68), "ton": 90, "commodity": "Soybeans", "color": "#2ecc71"},
    {"name": "Campo Largo", "coords": (-33.88, -60.85), "ton": 110, "commodity": "Wheat", "color": "#e67e22"},
    {"name": "Las Lilas", "coords": (-34.08, -60.38), "ton": 35, "commodity": "Maize", "color": "#f1c40f"},
    {"name": "El Amanecer", "coords": (-33.72, -60.55), "ton": 55, "commodity": "Soybeans", "color": "#2ecc71"}
]

print(f"Successfully initialized {len(farms_data)} supply nodes across 3 commodity categories.")


Successfully initialized 8 supply nodes across 3 commodity categories.

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
section: 4 Initialize Map (High-Contrast Dark Mode for Vector Clarity)

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
section: 5 Routing Engine: Pathfinding and Kinetic Mapping (AntPath)
text:
5 Routing Engine: Pathfinding and Kinetic Mapping (AntPath)
Calculating Dijkstra’s shortest path between Farm Nodes and the Central Plant.


Review Technical Code (Python)
# 1. Calculating Dijkstra's shortest path between Farm Nodes and the Central Plant.

for farm in farms_data:
    try:
        # Snap farm coordinates to the nearest network node
        farm_node = ox.nearest_nodes(G, farm["coords"][1], farm["coords"][0])
        
        # Calculate optimal route based on distance (length)
        optimal_path = nx.shortest_path(G, farm_node, target_plant_node, weight='length')
        
        # Generate geometry coordinates: [Origin] -> [Road Segments]
        route_geometry = [list(farm["coords"])] + [[G.nodes[node]['y'], G.nodes[node]['x']] for node in optimal_path]
        
        # Deploy Animated Polyline (Weight proportional to Load Capacity)
        AntPath(
            locations=route_geometry,
            color=farm["color"],
            pulse_color='#ffffff',
            weight=farm["ton"]/8,  # Dynamic line weight based on cargo volume
            delay=1100,
            tooltip=f"<b>{farm['name']}</b><br>Commodity: {farm['commodity']}<br>Load: {farm['ton']} MT"
        ).add_to(commodity_layers[farm["commodity"]])
        
        # Source Marker (Origin Point)
        folium.Marker(
            location=farm["coords"],
            icon=folium.Icon(color='black', icon_color=farm["color"], icon='leaf', prefix='fa'),
            popup=farm["name"]
        ).add_to(commodity_layers[farm["commodity"]])
        
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error processing route for {farm['name']}: {e}")
        continue


Error processing route for Don Pedro: No path between 4052042735 and 8039791631.

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
text:
6 Logistical Infrastructure and Control Interface


Review Technical Code (Python)
from branca.element import Figure
import os

# 1 UI Sizing (800px vertical expansion)
fig = Figure(height=800)

# 2 Cleanup: Removing duplicate LayerControls before final render
for key in list(m._children.keys()):
    if 'layer_control' in key:
        del m._children[key]

# 3 Destination Node: Central Processing Plant
folium.Marker(
    location=center_coords,
    icon=folium.Icon(color='red', icon='industry', prefix='fa'),
    popup="CENTRAL PROCESSING PLANT"
).add_to(m)

# 4 Single Instance Layer Control
folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False).add_to(m)

# 5 Encapsulation
fig.add_child(m)

# 6 FORCED EXPORT SYSTEM
# We define the absolute path to avoid Windows permission issues
filename = "day2_lines_antpath.html"
current_dir = os.getcwd()
full_path = os.path.join(current_dir, filename)

try:
    # Force save
    m.save(full_path)
    
    # 7 Verification Check
    if os.path.exists(full_path):
        print(f"✅ SUCCESS: File generated")
    else:
        print("❌ ERROR: The file was not found after the save command.")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"❌ CRITICAL ERROR during save: {e}")

# 8 Final Render for Notebook
fig


✅ SUCCESS: File generated

title: Agro-Logistics Dynamics: Multi-Commodity Flow Analysis
text:
7 Strategic Findings and Analytical Conclusions
The implementation of a dynamic routing model for multi-commodity agricultural flows provides critical insights into the regional supply chain. By analyzing the animated transit corridors between the storage units (Farms) and the processing hub, several logistical patterns emerge.

7.1 Key Analytical Insights

Corridor Pressure Analysis: The use of weighted polylines (proportional to tonnage) reveals the “High-Load Corridors.” Identifying these segments is vital for local infrastructure maintenance, as they bear the highest mechanical stress from heavy-duty grain transport.
Dynamic Flow Directionality: Utilizing AntPath allows for the visualization of real-time “pulse” in the network. This differentiates between static infrastructure and active movement, providing a more intuitive understanding of harvest-time logistics.
Multi-Commodity Convergence: The spatial intersection of Maize, Soybeans, and Wheat routes highlights potential bottlenecks. Strategic planning at these convergence points can significantly reduce transit latency and fuel consumption.



7.2 Technical Summary

Network Fidelity: Leveraging OSMNX ensured that the shortest path calculations were based on the actual topology of the driving network, providing a 1:1 reflection of field operations.
Scalable UI: The integration of a custom CSS header and a high-contrast Dark Matter base map enhances the legibility of vector data, making the dashboard suitable for executive-level reporting.
Optimization Potential: This model serves as a foundation for future Last-Mile Optimization and Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) simulations within the regional agricultural sector.


Developed by Alexis D. Araujo C. | Agricultural Engineer & Geospatial Data Scientist

title: Grain Storage Network Optimization
text:
In the global agribusiness sector, logistical efficiency is the primary driver of profitability. Beyond mere transportation, the strategic placement of post-harvest infrastructure determines the resilience of the entire supply chain. By leveraging Geographic Information Systems (GIS), we can analyze the spatial distribution of collection centers (silos) to minimize “farm-to-gate” latency and optimize transport overhead from harvest zones.
This project demonstrates an advanced workflow for the visualization and spatial auditing of a regional grain storage network.



A geographic imbalance between high-yield production zones and available storage capacity leads to critical systemic failures:

Operational Bottlenecks: Reception delays during peak harvest windows.
Carbon Footprint Escalation: Increased greenhouse gas emissions due to sub-optimal haulage distances.
Quantitative & Qualitative Loss: Post-harvest spoilage resulting from infrastructure gaps and inadequate storage conditions.




To address these challenges, this analysis implements three core geospatial techniques:

Proportional Symbolization: Mapping storage capacity through graduated point markers to visualize volumetric data.
Multivariate Categorization: Utilizing color-coded visual variables to differentiate between management models (State, Private, and Cooperative).
Spatial Density Analysis: Implementing Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) via Heatmaps to identify infrastructure saturation zones and underserved agricultural frontiers.




The solution is built on the Python Data Science ecosystem, ensuring a reproducible and scalable workflow:







Library
Application




Pandas & NumPy
High-performance data manipulation and synthetic attribute generation.


GeoPandas
Spatial operations, coordinate reference system (CRS) management, and vector processing.


Matplotlib
Production of high-fidelity static cartography and thematic layers.


Folium / Leaflet
Deployment of web-based interactive dashboards for stakeholder engagement.

title: Grain Storage Network Optimization
text:
2 Data Selection and Synthetic Generation
To simulate a realistic regional grain storage network, we generate a synthetic dataset representing 100 strategic collection points. This approach allows us to model a variety of management structures and volumetric capacities within a defined agricultural frontier.

2.1 Geographic Data Synthesis
We initialize the dataset with random spatial coordinates (localized within the Southern Cone, e.g., Argentina) and assign multivariate attributes to each infrastructure point.


Review Technical Code (Python)
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import numpy as np
from shapely.geometry import Point




Review Technical Code (Python)
# Configuration: Define the number of silos for the regional model
n_silos = 100

# Attribute Generation: 
# - Capacity: Volumetric storage in Metric Tons (MT)
# - Management Type: Operational model classification
# - Spatial Coordinates: Lat/Lon within the target agricultural zone
data = {
    'id': range(n_silos),
    'capacity_mt': np.random.randint(5000, 50000, n_silos),
    'management_type': np.random.choice(['Private', 'Cooperative', 'State-Owned'], n_silos),
    'lat': np.random.uniform(-35, -30, n_silos),
    'lon': np.random.uniform(-62, -58, n_silos)
}

# DataFrame Construction
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Vectorization: Creating Point geometries from coordinates
geometry = [Point(xy) for xy in zip(df.lon, df.lat)]

# GeoDataFrame Initialization: Set initial CRS to WGS84 (Geographic)
silos_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(df, geometry=geometry, crs="EPSG:4326")

# Preview the first 5 records of the spatial collection
silos_gdf.head(5)

2.2 Coordinate Reference System (CRS) Transformation
Standard geographic coordinates (WGS84) are ideal for global positioning but unsuitable for precise distance and area calculations. To enable metric-based spatial analysis, we re-project the dataset to a Projected Coordinate System (UTM Zone 20S).


Review Technical Code (Python)
# Re-projection: Transforming to UTM (EPSG:32720) for metric-based calculations
silos_metric = silos_gdf.to_crs(epsg=32720)

title: Grain Storage Network Optimization
text:
3 Effective Symbolization
To transform raw geographic data into actionable intelligence, we apply Multivariate Mapping techniques. This allows us to visualize two distinct variables simultaneously:
Volumetric Capacity (Quantitative): Represented through Proportional Symbols (Marker Size). Larger points indicate higher storage potential.
Management Model (Qualitative): Represented through Color Categorization (Hue). This differentiates the operational nature of each facility.


Review Technical Code (Python)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Initialize the figure with a professional aspect ratio
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

# 1. Execute Multivariate Symbolization
silos_gdf.plot(
    ax=ax,
    # Variable 1: Proportional scaling (Capacity in Metric Tons)
    # Dividing by 100 normalizes the point size for optimal map legibility
    markersize=silos_gdf['capacity_mt'] / 100, 
    
    # Variable 2: Categorical classification (Management Model)
    column='management_type', 
    
    legend=True,
    alpha=0.6,
    edgecolor='black',
    cmap='Set2'
)

# 2. Apply formal cartographic metadata
ax.set_title("Grain Storage Infrastructure: Capacity and Ownership Distribution", 
             fontsize=16, pad=20)
ax.set_xlabel("Longitude")
ax.set_ylabel("Latitude")

plt.show()

title: Grain Storage Network Optimization
Grain Storage Network Optimization
text:
5 Strategic Findings and Analytical Conclusions

5.1 UX/UI & Cartographic Efficiency
Dynamic Information Layering: The implementation of a toggleable legend via custom JavaScript injection solves a core limitation in standard Folium builds. This allows stakeholders to minimize cognitive load by clearing the canvas for unobstructed visual analysis while maintaining access to technical metadata (CRS: EPSG:32720) on demand.
Visual Hierarchy: Utilizing high-contrast stroke borders (weight=1) and a custom hexadecimal palette ensures that individual infrastructure points remain legible even within high-intensity heatmap zones, effectively neutralizing visual noise.


5.2 Multi-Scalar Spatial Intelligence
Macro-to-Micro Workflow: By layering Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) with discrete vector markers, the dashboard enables a seamless transition from Regional Cluster Analysis (identifying saturation zones) to Granular Asset Auditing (querying specific silo capacity and management models) within a single unified environment.


5.3 Agro-Industrial Impact & Scalability
Infrastructure Gap Detection: This tool serves as a primary Decision-Support System (DSS) for regional planning. It allows engineers to identify “blind spots”—agricultural frontiers where current storage capacity fails to meet projected harvest demand.
Logistical Optimization: The spatial distribution analysis directly informs the reduction of “farm-to-gate” distances, which is essential for lowering the carbon footprint and operational costs of the grain supply chain.

title: About Me
text:
Agricultural Engineer | Geospatial Data Scientist | AgTech Automator

I am a results-driven Agricultural Engineer with over a decade of experience in field operations and digital agriculture. My mission is to bridge the gap between traditional agronomy and modern data science by leveraging Python, QGIS, and Geospatial Analysis to optimize decision-making in the field.



Geospatial Analysis: Expert in QGIS, GeoPandas, and OSMnx for infrastructure mapping and route optimization.
Data Automation: Specialized in streamlining agricultural data collection and processing using Python (Pandas, Folium, Matplotlib).
Field Operations: Extensive background in Forestry Supervision and Crop Monitoring, ensuring logistics and production targets are met with precision.





Agroescagüey Agricultural Production Unit (2017 – 2026): Focused on implementing digital agriculture tools and automated data collection systems to enhance crop yields and monitoring efficiency.
Terranova de Venezuela (2012 – 2016): Supervised forest operations and timber logistics, managing complex geospatial planning for infrastructure development.





Agricultural Engineering
Python for Everybody Specialization – University of Michigan (Coursera)
